\title{From Per-Row Launches to a Latency-Hidden Single-Block Dynamic Program:\\ Optimizing Vertical Seam Carving on a V100 GPU\\
{\footnotesize Final Project --- Parallel Programming, Spring 2026}}

\maketitle

%------------------------------------------------------------------------------
\begin{abstract}
Seam carving is a content-aware image-resizing technique whose core is a
row-by-row dynamic program (DP) with a strict top-to-bottom dependency, making
it awkward to parallelize on a GPU. We build a standalone CUDA implementation of
vertical seam carving for an NVIDIA V100 and optimize the dominant DP kernel
through a naive baseline (v1) and five successive versions (v2--v6). Starting
from the naive baseline, which launches
one kernel per image row, we fuse the entire DP into a single thread block that
keeps two cost rows in shared memory, then attack the resulting latency wall:
Nsight Compute shows the fused kernel is bound by global-load latency on the
energy map, not by compute or barriers. Software-pipelined prefetching of the
energy rows removes that stall, and packing the back-pointers into one signed
byte cuts store traffic fourfold. A final templated generalization lifts a hard
width limit so 4K images run. Measured end-to-end on three resolutions
(1428$\times$968 to 3840$\times$2160), the optimized kernel is
\textbf{16--24$\times$} faster than a single-threaded CPU baseline and
\textbf{2.2--3.1$\times$} faster than the naive GPU baseline, the CPU margin
widening on larger images. We also report a counter-intuitive result: fusing the
DP---worth $1.6$--$1.7\times$ on the smaller images---yields essentially no gain
at 4K, where only latency hiding delivers a clear win.
\end{abstract}

\begin{IEEEkeywords}
seam carving, dynamic programming, CUDA, GPU optimization, memory latency, V100
\end{IEEEkeywords}

\vspace{0.5em}
\noindent\fbox{\parbox{0.97\columnwidth}{\textbf{Project Category:}\quad
$\boxtimes$~\textbf{A} (Self-chosen topic)\qquad
$\square$~\textbf{B} (AI-agent code optimization)}}

%------------------------------------------------------------------------------
\section{Introduction}
\emph{Seam carving}~\cite{avidan2007} resizes an image by repeatedly removing a
\emph{seam}: a connected path of pixels, one per row, that crosses the least
``important'' content. Importance is an \emph{energy} map (here a gradient
magnitude), and the minimum-energy seam is found with a dynamic program (DP)
that accumulates cost from the top row to the bottom. Figure~\ref{fig:carve}
shows the effect: removing many low-energy vertical seams narrows the image
while preserving salient structure. Removing $k$ seams repeats the whole
pipeline $k$ times.

The DP is the hard part to parallelize. Each output row depends on the row
directly above it, so the $H$ rows must be processed in order---an irreducible
serial chain for an \emph{exact} result. Within a row, however, all $W$ columns
are independent. A natural but naive GPU implementation launches one kernel per
row; this pays kernel-launch and global-memory round-trip costs $H$ times per
seam. The question this project asks is: \emph{how fast can the exact seam-carve
DP run on one GPU, and what actually limits it?}

We answer it by building a 100\%-custom CUDA pipeline (no deep-learning
framework) for an NVIDIA V100 and optimizing the DP kernel over a naive baseline
(v1) through five successive versions (v2--v6), each isolating one idea so its
effect is measurable. Our contribution
is threefold: (i) a fused single-block DP that turns $H$ launches into one;
(ii) a profiling-driven diagnosis that the fused kernel is \emph{global-load
latency bound}, leading to a software-pipelined prefetch and a 1-byte
back-pointer encoding that together give another $1.7$--$2.1\times$; and
(iii) an honest scaling study across three resolutions and a CPU baseline that
surfaces a non-obvious lesson---fusion is not universally beneficial, and the
win at 4K comes entirely from latency hiding.

\begin{figure*}[t]
  \centering
  % Align the three panels by HEIGHT (seam carving keeps height fixed and only
  % narrows the width), so panel (c) correctly appears narrower than (a)/(b).
  \newlength{\carvefigh}\setlength{\carvefigh}{3.1cm}
  \begin{minipage}[b]{0.33\textwidth}\centering
    \includegraphics[height=\carvefigh]{figures/broadway_orig.jpg}\\[2pt]
    {\footnotesize (a) Original}
  \end{minipage}\hfill
  \begin{minipage}[b]{0.33\textwidth}\centering
    \includegraphics[height=\carvefigh]{figures/broadway_seams.png}\\[2pt]
    {\footnotesize (b) Removed seams (red)}
  \end{minipage}\hfill
  \begin{minipage}[b]{0.33\textwidth}\centering
    \includegraphics[height=\carvefigh]{figures/broadway_carved.png}\\[2pt]
    {\footnotesize (c) After carving}
  \end{minipage}
  \caption{Content-aware resizing on Broadway (1428$\times$968). (a) input;
  (b) the low-energy vertical seams our DP removes, painted red on the original;
  (c) the narrowed result---the tower is preserved while low-energy regions are
  collapsed. Generated by \texttt{seam\_vis} (same algorithm as the GPU path).}
  \label{fig:carve}
\end{figure*}

%------------------------------------------------------------------------------
\section{Background and Related Work}
\textbf{Seam carving.} Avidan and Shamir~\cite{avidan2007} introduced
content-aware resizing. For an $H\times W$ gray image with energy $e[i][j]$, the
cumulative cost of the minimum vertical seam ending at pixel $(i,j)$ is
\begin{equation}
M[i][j] = e[i][j] + \min\!\big(M[i\!-\!1][j\!-\!1],\,M[i\!-\!1][j],\,M[i\!-\!1][j\!+\!1]\big),
\label{eq:dp}
\end{equation}
with $M[0][j]=e[0][j]$. The seam is recovered by taking the $\arg\min$ over the
last row and back-tracking the stored parent choices. We resolve ties
left$\rightarrow$center$\rightarrow$right (strict ``$<$''); fixing this order
makes the chosen seam deterministic, so every version below produces the
identical result and can be compared bit-for-bit.

\textbf{Target hardware.} The V100 (Volta, \texttt{sm\_70}) has 80 streaming
multiprocessors (SMs), up to 1024 threads per block, and up to 96\,KB of
opt-in dynamic shared memory per block~\cite{voltawp}. These three numbers shape
every decision below: 80 SMs is the parallelism a kernel should try to fill, and
96\,KB bounds how wide an image a single block can hold two cost rows for.

\textbf{Why not just use more blocks?} Equation~\eqref{eq:dp}'s row dependency
means a multi-block DP needs a grid-wide barrier after every row. We tried this
(cooperative groups, \texttt{grid.sync()} per row) and it regressed
(Section~\ref{sec:method}); the barrier cost across $H$ rows outweighs the extra
SMs. This motivates the single-block design and the latency-hiding focus.

%------------------------------------------------------------------------------
\section{Methodology}
\label{sec:method}
The pipeline has four custom kernels: grayscale, energy (clamped
$|{\nabla_x}|+|{\nabla_y}|$), the cost DP, and seam removal
(Fig.~\ref{fig:pipeline}). Profiling (Section~\ref{sec:setup}) confirms the DP is
$\sim$93\% of per-seam time on a 1428$\times$968 image; the other three kernels
are cheap and memory-bound as expected. All optimization therefore targets the
DP. Each version is a separate source file and binary so earlier versions stay
intact for A/B comparison.

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\begin{tikzpicture}[font=\footnotesize,node distance=3mm and 4mm]
  \tikzstyle{k}=[draw,rounded corners,minimum height=6mm,inner sep=2pt,align=center]
  \node[k] (g)  {grayscale};
  \node[k,right=of g] (e) {energy};
  \node[k,right=of e,fill=blue!12,very thick] (d) {DP cost\\\textbf{$\sim$93\%}};
  \node[k,right=of d] (r) {remove\\seam};
  \draw[-{Latex}] (g)--(e); \draw[-{Latex}] (e)--(d); \draw[-{Latex}] (d)--(r);
  \draw[-{Latex}] (r.south) .. controls +(0,-6mm) and +(0,-6mm) .. (g.south)
    node[midway,below,font=\scriptsize] {repeat $k$ times (one seam each)};
  % DP dependency inset
  \begin{scope}[shift={(0,-2.2)}]
    \foreach \x in {0,1,2} \node[draw,minimum size=4.2mm] (a\x) at (\x*0.5,0.5) {};
    \node[draw,minimum size=4.2mm,fill=blue!12] (b1) at (0.5,0) {};
    \draw[-{Latex}] (a0.south) -- (b1.north west);
    \draw[-{Latex}] (a1.south) -- (b1.north);
    \draw[-{Latex}] (a2.south) -- (b1.north east);
    \node[right=5mm of a2,align=left] {row $i\!-\!1$ (in shared mem)\\$M[i][j]\!=\!e[i][j]\!+\!\min(\cdot,\cdot,\cdot)$\\row $i$ depends on 3 cells above};
  \end{scope}
\end{tikzpicture}
\caption{The four-kernel pipeline (top) and the DP's 3-way row dependency
(bottom). The $H$ rows are serial; the $W$ columns within a row are parallel.}
\label{fig:pipeline}
\end{figure}

\textbf{v1 --- naive baseline.} The DP is one kernel launch per row: $H{-}1$
launches per seam, a full $H\times W$ cost matrix and back-pointer matrix in
global memory, no reuse. It isolates exactly the cost that fusion removes---a
per-row launch and a full global round-trip for every row.

\textbf{v2 --- fused single-block DP.} One thread block walks all rows
top-to-bottom. The \emph{previous} and \emph{current} cost rows live in shared
memory and ping-pong each iteration; a \texttt{\_\_syncthreads()} separates
rows. One launch replaces $H$. Threads stride over the $W$ columns. This is the
structural win over v1.

\textbf{v3 --- multi-block, \texttt{grid.sync()} (abandoned).} To use more than
one SM we made the DP a cooperative-groups kernel with a grid-wide barrier per
row. It \emph{regressed} (Section~\ref{sec:results}): Nsight shows it becomes
barrier-bound (dominant \texttt{barrier} stall, $\sim$6\% occupancy). Confirming
the Section~II hypothesis, we abandoned this direction---the lever is not more
SMs but a cheaper per-row step.

\textbf{v4 --- software-pipelined energy prefetch.} Nsight Compute identifies
the v2 bottleneck as the \texttt{long\_scoreboard} warp stall: every warp stalls
on the global load of the current row's energy, and the serial dependency hides
nothing. We prefetch row $i{+}1$'s energy into registers \emph{while} computing
row $i$, overlapping the global load with arithmetic. Each thread owns up to two
columns (so $W\le 2048$), kept register-resident.

\textbf{v5 --- 1-byte back-pointers.} The back-pointer per cell is one of three
relative choices ($-1,0,+1$); v2/v4 store it as a 4-byte \texttt{int}. We store
a \texttt{signed char} instead, cutting back-store traffic $4\times$;
back-tracking adds the offset. Output is bit-identical.

\textbf{v6 --- width generalization.} The $W\le 2048$ cap in v4/v5 is the
hard-coded ``two columns per thread,'' not a hardware limit; the real ceiling is
shared memory ($2{\cdot}W{\cdot}4\le 96$\,KB). v6 makes the columns-per-thread a
compile-time template parameter and the host dispatches on
$\lceil W/\text{blockDim}\rceil$; the fixed-size, fully unrolled prefetch arrays
stay in registers (no local-memory spill), preserving v5's behavior. For
$W\le 2048$ it is byte-identical to v5; 3840$\times$2160 uses four columns per
thread. This is what lets all three resolutions run.

%------------------------------------------------------------------------------
\section{Experimental Setup}
\label{sec:setup}
\textbf{Hardware.} NVIDIA V100 (\texttt{sm\_70}, 80 SMs, 96\,KB shared memory
opt-in) on the TWCC Taiwania-2 cluster, jobs placed by SLURM. The CPU baseline
uses the 4 host cores co-allocated with one GPU on that partition.

\textbf{Software.} CUDA 12.8, \texttt{nvcc -O3 -arch=sm\_70}. Image I/O via the
header-only \texttt{stb\_image}.

\textbf{CPU baseline.} For a host-side reference we wrote the same four-stage
pipeline in plain C++ (\texttt{g++ -O3 -march=native}); it mirrors the CUDA
kernels bit-for-bit---identical grayscale, energy, DP recurrence
(Eq.~\eqref{eq:dp}), and left$\rightarrow$center$\rightarrow$right tie-break---so
its output matches the GPU exactly. An OpenMP variant (\texttt{-fopenmp},
\texttt{OMP\_NUM\_THREADS=4}) parallelizes the three data-parallel passes
(grayscale, energy, seam removal) across image rows with \texttt{\#pragma omp
parallel for}. The DP keeps its $H$ rows serial---the row dependency is the same
one that constrains the GPU---and instead parallelizes the $W$ cells \emph{within}
each row. That choice forks and joins a thread team once per row ($H$ times per
seam) which, together with the memory-bound access pattern, is why four cores
deliver only $\sim$3$\times$ over one (Sec.~\ref{sec:disc}), not the ideal
4$\times$.

\textbf{Measurement.} We time the whole carving loop (all four kernels, all
seams): \texttt{cudaEvent} on the GPU, \texttt{std::chrono} on the CPU. We
report \emph{best-of-3} runs as \emph{ms per seam}. Kernel-level profiling uses
Nsight Compute (\texttt{ncu}): duration, SM/memory throughput, achieved
occupancy, and the top warp-stall reasons.

\textbf{Workload.} Three images spanning $6\times$ in pixel count---Broadway
(1428$\times$968, 1.38\,MP), input1 (1920$\times$1080, 2.07\,MP), input
(3840$\times$2160, 8.29\,MP). For the version sweep we remove 5\%, 10\%, and
20\% of the width to test scaling in the number of seams (full table in the
Appendix); the CPU comparison and Table~\ref{tab:e2e} use a fixed 10 seams
because the single-threaded baseline is slow. The two protocols give slightly
different ms/seam for the same system (e.g.\ v6 on Broadway is 0.89 at 10 seams
vs.\ 0.74 averaged over a 5--20\% sweep) because a fixed one-time GPU warm-up
inside the timed region amortizes over fewer seams at 10; all speedup ratios are
computed within one protocol, so they remain internally consistent.

\textbf{Correctness.} Outputs are compared with \texttt{cmp}; on the resolutions
each version supports, all (v1--v6) are bit-identical (the deterministic
tie-break guarantees a unique seam; v5 is limited to $W\le2048$). Correctness is
thus preserved by construction across every optimization.

%------------------------------------------------------------------------------
\section{Results}
\label{sec:results}
\textbf{Kernel-level effect of each version.} Table~\ref{tab:versions} and
Fig.~\ref{fig:versions} summarize the DP kernel on the 1428$\times$968 image
(Nsight, first seam). Fusion (v2) is the structural baseline; the multi-SM v3
regresses; prefetch (v4) removes the dominant \texttt{long\_scoreboard} stall
and gives the single largest jump; the 1-byte back-pointer (v5) trims store
traffic to reach the latency floor of the serial row chain, where no single
stall reason dominates.

\begin{table}[t]
\caption{DP kernel per version (1428$\times$968, Nsight, first seam). v1 is the
naive baseline---$H$ per-row launches, with no single fused kernel to profile;
its end-to-end cost is in Table~\ref{tab:e2e}.}
\label{tab:versions}
\centering
\footnotesize
\begin{tabular}{@{}llll@{}}
\toprule
Ver. & DP dur. (ms) & vs v2 & Key technique \\
\midrule
v1 & ---   & ---          & naive: $H$ per-row launches (no fused kernel) \\
v2 & 1.46  & 1.00$\times$ & fused single-block, shared-mem ping-pong \\
v3 & 1.66  & 0.88$\times$ & \texttt{grid.sync()} per row (regressed) \\
v4 & 0.872 & 1.67$\times$ & software-pipelined energy prefetch \\
v5 & 0.790 & 1.85$\times$ & + 1-byte relative back-pointers \\
v6 & 0.790 & 1.85$\times$ & + width generalization (templated) \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\begin{tikzpicture}
\begin{axis}[
  width=\columnwidth, height=4.2cm,
  ybar, bar width=10pt,
  symbolic x coords={v2,v3,v4,v5,v6},
  xtick=data, ymin=0, ymax=2.3,
  ylabel={DP duration (ms)}, ylabel near ticks,
  nodes near coords, nodes near coords style={font=\scriptsize},
  every node near coord/.append style={/pgf/number format/fixed,
     /pgf/number format/precision=2},
  enlarge x limits=0.12,
]
\addplot[fill=blue!25] coordinates {(v2,1.46)(v3,1.66)(v4,0.872)(v5,0.790)(v6,0.790)};
\node[red,font=\scriptsize\bfseries] at (axis cs:v3,2.05) {regressed};
\end{axis}
\end{tikzpicture}
\caption{Fused DP-kernel duration per version (v1 naive omitted---per-row
launches, no single kernel). v3 (multi-SM \texttt{grid.sync}) is slower than the
fused v2; prefetch (v4) is the largest single drop.}
\label{fig:versions}
\end{figure}

\textbf{End-to-end across resolutions and baselines.}
Table~\ref{tab:e2e} gives whole-pipeline ms/seam (best-of-3, 10 seams) for the
CPU baselines, the naive GPU (v1), the fused GPU (v2), and the optimized GPU
(v6); Fig.~\ref{fig:speedup} plots v6's speedup over each. The optimized kernel
is \textbf{16--24$\times$} a single CPU core and \textbf{5.9--7.6$\times$} four
OpenMP cores; against the naive GPU baseline it is \textbf{2.2--3.1$\times$}.
Crucially, the GPU advantage over the CPU \emph{grows} with image size
(16.2$\to$23.9$\times$), because larger rows expose more column parallelism to
fill the 80 SMs.

\begin{table}[t]
\caption{End-to-end ms/seam, best-of-3, 10 seams. Lower is better.}
\label{tab:e2e}
\centering
\footnotesize
\begin{tabular}{@{}lccccccc@{}}
\toprule
Image & CPU & CPU & v1 & v2 & v6 & v6 vs & v6 vs \\
(res) & 1\,thr & omp4 & naive & fuse & best & CPU1 & v1 \\
\midrule
1428$\times$968   & 14.38 & 5.28  & 2.71 & 1.59 & 0.89 & 16.2$\times$ & 3.1$\times$ \\
1920$\times$1080  & 21.15 & 7.13  & 3.12 & 1.94 & 1.12 & 18.8$\times$ & 2.8$\times$ \\
3840$\times$2160  & 75.15 & 23.77 & 6.79 & 6.73 & 3.14 & 23.9$\times$ & 2.2$\times$ \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\begin{tikzpicture}
\begin{axis}[
  width=\columnwidth, height=4.6cm,
  ybar, bar width=5pt, ymode=log, log origin=infty,
  symbolic x coords={r1,r2,r3},
  xtick=data, xticklabels={1428x968,1920x1080,3840x2160},
  x tick label style={font=\scriptsize},
  ymin=1, ymax=40, ylabel={v6 speedup ($\times$, log)}, ylabel near ticks,
  legend style={font=\scriptsize,at={(0.5,1.02)},anchor=south,legend columns=4,
     /tikz/every even column/.append style={column sep=4pt}},
  enlarge x limits=0.22,
]
\addplot[fill=red!30]    coordinates {(r1,16.2)(r2,18.8)(r3,23.9)};
\addplot[fill=orange!40] coordinates {(r1,5.93)(r2,6.35)(r3,7.57)};
\addplot[fill=blue!30]   coordinates {(r1,3.05)(r2,2.78)(r3,2.16)};
\addplot[fill=green!35]  coordinates {(r1,1.79)(r2,1.73)(r3,2.14)};
\legend{vs CPU1,vs CPU x4,vs v1,vs v2}
\end{axis}
\end{tikzpicture}
\caption{Speedup of the optimized GPU kernel (v6) over each baseline. The CPU
margin grows with image size; the ``vs v1'' and ``vs v2'' bars nearly coincide
at 4K, where fusion alone barely helps and latency hiding carries the win.}
\label{fig:speedup}
\end{figure}

\textbf{Scaling in seams and pixels.} Across 5\%/10\%/20\% width removal, v6's
ms/seam is constant to within a few percent ($\sim$4\%) on each image, so total
time is essentially linear in the number of seams. Across images (Fig.~\ref{fig:scaling}), v6's
ms/seam grows \emph{sublinearly} with pixel count: a $6.0\times$ increase in
pixels (1.38\,$\to$\,8.29\,MP) costs only $\sim$4.1$\times$ more time
(0.74\,$\to$\,3.01\,ms/seam), so the per-pixel cost actually improves on larger
images as the pipeline's fixed per-seam overheads amortize. v6's slope stays well
below v2's throughout.

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\begin{tikzpicture}
\begin{axis}[
  width=\columnwidth, height=4.4cm,
  xlabel={image size (megapixels)}, ylabel={ms / seam},
  xlabel near ticks, ylabel near ticks,
  xmin=0.5, xmax=9, ymin=0, ymax=7,
  legend pos=north west, legend style={font=\scriptsize},
  grid=major, grid style={gray!20},
]
\addplot[mark=square*,blue,thick]  coordinates {(1.38,1.376)(2.07,1.714)(8.29,6.220)};
\addplot[mark=*,red,thick]         coordinates {(1.38,0.740)(2.07,0.942)(8.29,3.010)};
\legend{v2 (fused),v6 (optimized)}
\end{axis}
\end{tikzpicture}
\caption{ms/seam vs.\ image size. Both scale sublinearly with pixel count (6$\times$
more pixels, $\sim$4$\times$ more time), but v6's slope is roughly half of
v2's---the latency-hiding savings grow with the DP's memory traffic.}
\label{fig:scaling}
\end{figure}

%------------------------------------------------------------------------------
\section{Discussion and Analysis}
\label{sec:disc}
\textbf{Why fusion is not free.} The most informative result is the 4K row of
Table~\ref{tab:e2e}: at 3840$\times$2160 the fused v2 (6.73) is no faster than
the naive v1 (6.79)---a $\sim$1.0$\times$ ``speedup''---whereas on the two
smaller images fusion gives $1.70\times$ and $1.61\times$ (v1$/$v2). Folding the
whole DP into a single block means one SM, with 1024 threads, chewing a
3840-wide row; its throughput cannot beat the naive kernel, whose \emph{per-row}
launch still spreads $W$ columns across all 80 SMs. Fusion's benefit---eliminating
$H$ launches---is large only when launch/round-trip cost is a sizable fraction of
the per-row work, which holds for narrow images but not for a 3840-wide one. The
lesson: \emph{profile before assuming fusion wins}.

\textbf{Why the single-block design wins anyway.} What rescues the fused path at
4K is latency hiding, not parallelism. v4's prefetch overlaps the energy load
with arithmetic; v5 quarters the back-store volume. Together they take v6 to
$2.2\times$ over v1 and $2.1\times$ over v2 at 4K (Table~\ref{tab:e2e};
$6.79/3.14=2.16$, $6.73/3.14=2.14$), converting fusion's break-even into a clear
win.
The serial $H$-row chain is irreducible, so the only lever is making each
row-step cheaper---exactly what v4/v5 do, and exactly why the multi-SM v3 (which
instead adds barrier cost) failed.

\textbf{Where it breaks down (limitations).} (1) The single block uses one SM,
so 79/80 of the GPU is idle during the DP; for very wide images this caps
throughput---a batched or wavefront scheme that tiles multiple SMs while
amortizing barriers is the natural next step, at the cost of halo redundancy.
(2) Shared memory bounds width to $\sim$12\,K columns ($2{\cdot}W{\cdot}4\le
96$\,KB); beyond that the two cost rows no longer fit. (3) We remove seams one at
a time (exact); approximate multi-seam removal could amortize the energy/DP
recompute but would change the output. (4) The CPU OpenMP baseline scales only
$\sim$3$\times$ on 4 cores, limited by per-row fork--join and memory bandwidth,
so it is a conservative---not adversarial---CPU point.

\textbf{Threats to validity.} Numbers are best-of-3 wall-clock on a shared
cluster; we mitigate jitter with best-of-N and by reporting ms/seam. The v1
baseline is a deliberately lean one-launch-per-row kernel; a less careful naive
implementation (extra per-row work or a host synchronization between rows) would
only widen the gap, so our speedups over ``naive'' are conservative.

%------------------------------------------------------------------------------
\section{Conclusion}
We optimized the exact vertical seam-carving DP on a V100 from a per-row-launch
baseline to a latency-hidden single-block kernel, reaching 16--24$\times$ over a
single CPU core (the margin growing on larger images) and 2.2--3.1$\times$ over
a naive GPU baseline. The decisive insight was diagnostic rather than
algorithmic: the fused kernel is bound by global-load latency, not compute or
barriers, so prefetching and a compact back-pointer encoding---not more
SMs---deliver the speedup. The study also documents a useful negative result:
fusion's benefit vanishes at 4K (a wash against the naive baseline), and only
latency hiding makes the single-block design a clear win. Future work is a
wavefront-tiled multi-SM DP that keeps the latency hiding while reclaiming the
idle 79 SMs.

\section{Full Multi-Resolution Sweep}
Table~\ref{tab:sweep} lists whole-pipeline ms/seam (best-of-3) for every
image $\times$ crop-ratio $\times$ version. v5 cannot run the 3840-wide image
(its $W\le2048$ cap), which motivated v6. ms/seam is nearly flat across crop
ratios, so total time is linear in the number of seams.

\begin{table}[h]
\caption{ms/seam by image, crop ratio, and version (best-of-3).}
\label{tab:sweep}
\centering
\footnotesize
\begin{tabular}{@{}llccc@{}}
\toprule
Image (res) & crop & v2 & v5 & v6 \\
\midrule
Broadway          & 5\%  & 1.3969 & 0.7857 & 0.7540 \\
1428$\times$968   & 10\% & 1.3827 & 0.7775 & 0.7431 \\
                  & 20\% & 1.3485 & 0.7627 & 0.7239 \\
\midrule
input1            & 5\%  & 1.7299 & 1.0187 & 0.9581 \\
1920$\times$1080  & 10\% & 1.7186 & 1.0048 & 0.9451 \\
                  & 20\% & 1.6954 & 0.9787 & 0.9234 \\
\midrule
input             & 5\%  & 6.2347 & ---    & 3.0691 \\
3840$\times$2160  & 10\% & 6.2038 & ---    & 3.0192 \\
                  & 20\% & 6.2217 & ---    & 2.9404 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\section{Reproduction}
Source, Makefile, and benchmark scripts are in the project repository. Build with
\texttt{module load cuda \&\& make}; the version sweep is \texttt{bash bench.sh},
and the CPU-vs-GPU comparison is \texttt{bash baseline\_compare.sh}. The
Figure~\ref{fig:carve} assets are produced by \texttt{make seam\_vis \&\& bash
gen\_figures.sh}. Per-version optimization notes are in
\texttt{cuda/OPTIMIZATION\_LOG.md}.

\end{document}
